title: Chocolate orange cake
subtitle: A two-layer chocolate sponge with dark orange ganache
excerpt: Two layers of chocolate sponge scented with orange zest, sandwiched and coated with a dark chocolate and orange ganache. The ganache sets softly and cuts clean.
sourceNotes: The combination of dark chocolate and orange in baked goods has been a fixture of British confectionery since Terry's Chocolate Orange was introduced in 1932, but it predates that in French patisserie (orange-flavoured ganache is documented in Escoffier). This recipe synthesises the home-baker layer-cake method found in Mary Berry's Complete Cookbook (1995) and Nigella Lawson's How to Be a Domestic Goddess (2000), with a ganache derived from the professional ratio of 2:1 cream to chocolate for a pourable consistency.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers, wrapped individually, for up to 2 months. Assemble and ganache after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponges up to 2 days ahead and store wrapped at room temperature. Ganache and assemble on the day of serving.

baking.decoratingTechnique: chocolate orange ganache poured over assembled cake, set at room temperature

glossaryTerms.definition: An emulsion of hot cream and chopped chocolate. At a 1:1 ratio of cream to chocolate, it sets firm enough to roll into truffles. At 2:1 cream to chocolate (used here), it stays pourable at room temperature and sets to a soft, glossy coating as it cools.

content.text: Two sponge layers of the same size, stacked with a thin film of ganache between them and covered in the rest. The ganache needs to be poured while still warm and liquid; once it begins to set, attempting to spread it tears the surface. Work quickly and let gravity do the covering.

Zest the oranges before juicing them. Rub the zest into the caster sugar with your fingertips before adding the butter: this bursts the oil cells and intensifies the flavour. Two oranges should give enough zest for the sponge and ganache.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Grease and line two 20 cm round cake tins. Rub the {{orange}} zest into the {{caster-sugar}} with your fingertips until fragrant. Beat in the {{unsalted-butter}} until pale and fluffy. Add the {{eggs}} one at a time, beating well after each. Sift together the {{plain-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} and fold in two additions, adding the {{whole-milk}} with the second. Divide between the tins and bake for 28 to 32 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Cool in tins for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack to cool completely.

Place the {{dark-chocolate}} in a heatproof bowl. Heat the {{double-cream}} in a small saucepan until just beginning to simmer, then pour over the chocolate. Add the {{orange-juice}}. Leave for 2 minutes, then stir from the centre outward until smooth. This is a

. Leave at room temperature for 20 to 30 minutes until it thickens to a pourable consistency. Place one sponge layer on a rack set over a tray. Spread 3 tablespoons of ganache over it, top with the second layer, and pour the remaining ganache over the top, letting it run down the sides naturally. Leave undisturbed for at least 30 minutes to set before cutting.
